coredata.dc:title: Non-linear ultrasonics to determine molecular properties of pure liquids
coredata.dc:creator: Sehgal, Chandra M.
coredata.dcterms:subject: non-linear ultrasonics
diffusion coefficients
molecular properties

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext articleinfo crossmark dco dateupdated tomb dateloaded datesearch indexeddate issuelist volumelist yearnav absattachment articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright dateloadedtxt docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid issfirst issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype volfirst volissue webpdf webpdfpagecount affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst pubtype ref alllist content subj ssids

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: Non-linear ultrasonics to determine molecular properties of pure liquids Chandra M. Sehgal University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, PA 19104, USA Revised 5 August 1994; accepted 12 September 1994 Wave propagation of ultrasound through condensed media is fundamentally non-linear in nature. The extent of non-linearity is related to the ratio of the third to second virial coefficients (B/A) of the equation of state of the medium. This ratio can be directly measured by determining the rate of change in sound speed with pressure. Several simple relations are developed that relate acoustic non-linearity to the molecular properties, namely internal pressure, free energy of binding, the effective van der Waals' constants, the translational diffusion coefficient, and the rotational correlation time. Using these relationships and the measured the acoustic non-linearity parameter and the sound speed, the molecular properties are determined for the liquids: water, alcohols and fluorocarbons. In all cases the values obtained from ultrasonic measurements compare closely with the values reported in the literature based on alternative measurements. In conclusion, the non-linear propagation of ultrasound offers an independent means of determining the molecular properties of condensed media. Keywords: non-linear ultrasonics; diffusion coefficients; molecular properties Different parts of the electromagnetic spectrum have been used to probe the physical state of matter - often defined by properties like intermolecular distances, the force and the energy binding the molecules together, the diffusion and rotational coefficients, the correlation times, the rate constants of equilibria and so on. In this regard ultrasound also offers a potential to explore molecular properties. This form of energy is not a part of the electromagnetic spectrum and so it interacts with matter differently than other forms of radiation. In contrast to electromagnetic radiation, which interacts with a medium directly through coupling with electrons and nuclei, high frequency ultrasonic waves interact indirectly, namely, by vibratory perturbations of the weak bonds between molecules. Earlier studies on chemical ultrasonics can be classified into two domains. In the linear domain it is assumed that the density of a medium changes in proportion to the acoustic pressure. This constraint is satisfied either when the medium behaves like an ideal gas, or when the pressure amplitude of the propagating wave is small enough to cause only weak perturbations in density. Within these limits, the speed of propagation and the absorption of ultrasonic waves have been used to investigate the compressibility of the medium and the relaxation of various chemical equilibria, respectively. The second area of investigation in chemical ultrasonics is in the domain of extreme non-linearities of wave propagation. In this regime ultrasonic propagation results in cavitation, shock waves, sonoluminescence and sonochemical reactions. Between the two extreme regimes of perfect linear wave propagation and that of 'chaos', characterized by the random nature of cavitation, is a regime of intermediate non-linearity. Within this regime, the acoustic wave propagation is well behaved, yet it does not follow a simple linear relation between pressure and density. This is particularly valid when the amplitude of the propagating wave is large and/or when ultrasonic energy propagates in a condensed medium. Although this regime of ultrasound-matter interaction has been well described in the discipline of acoustics1 its use to determine physical properties of matter has been limited to only a few studies2"9. The purpose of this paper is to determine the use of non-linear acoustics to explore the properties of pure liquids, namely, the equation of state, the internal pressure, the free energy of binding, the effective van der Waals' constants, translational diffusion coefficients and rotational correlation times. Equation of state and internal pressure Consider a medium of density p at a total pressure of P. This pressure is made up of two terms: the measurable hydrostatic pressure ?and the internal pressure of attraction, Pwhich molecules exert on one another. The latter is not a directly observable quantity. If the pressure P on the medium is changed to P' the pressure-density relationship can be expressed as (la) P_J^ P \p} where p' is the density at the pressure P' and the exponent 0041-624X/95/S09.50 1995 - Eisevier Science B.V. All rights reserved SS0041-624X (94)00014-X Ultrasonics 1995 Vol 33 No 2 155 Determination of molecular properties of liquids: C.M. Sehgal is a constant that depends on the nature of the medium and how the compression/expansion is carried out. For example, in the case of ideal gases ^ is equal to the specific heat ratio (y) for an adiabatic process, and is equal to unity for an isothermal process. It must be noted that the value of = y is only applicable to ideal gases. Its application to liquids is very restricted and is limited to a narrow range where the density excursions from the equilibrium values are extremely small. From a practical point of view, Equation (la) cannot be used to define the properties of liquids over an extended range if is assumed to be equal to y. It is the purpose of this paper to demonstrate that the applicability of Equation (la) can be greatly extended to include condensed media, if an appropriate value of the exponent is used. This value can be determined experimentally by using non-linear acoustics. To develop this relationship consider a medium of low compressibility (e.g. a liquid). For such a medium p' = p + Ap (Ib) where the change in density, Ap is small compared with the equilibrium value p, i.e. Ap < p. Using Equation (Ib) in Equation (la) and expanding the resulting equation by a binomial series, one obtains P^P^pf^'^pf^ \P 2! ^" P /s (2) where the subscript s represents isotropic pressure change. The generalized equation of state of a medium, obtained by the Taylor expansion of pressure as a function of density for the isentropic condition, is p..?,w-^U^) w-.f.. \8p/s,p 2\op2 /s,p Ul /Ap\ B/ApV P' =P + A -' +- +... \ph 2\p}s where , op /s,p B= pl\ .op2 (3a) (3b) (3c) (3d) The coefficients A and B are also temperature dependent. If Equations (la) and (2) are to represent a medium described by the generalized equation of state (Equation 3a), the following conditions must be satisfied, namely (4) A=^P B = ^ - 1)P (5) There are two consequences of this formalism. It implies that the coefficients A and B, which are supposed to define the pressure-density relationship, are themselves functions of pressure, P. Putting this differently, the pressure-density relationship of a mdium is not unique or fixed for all conditions, but varies as the pressure is altered. The second consequence is that the coefficients A and B are mutually dependent on each other. They are both functions of the same parameters and P. Taking the ratio of the two coefficients yields ^ = {B/A) + 1 (6) The exponent is a fully defined quantity provided the ratio of the coefficients is known. This ratio is directly related to sound propagation properties10, and can be determined by measuring sound speed and its pressure derivative5'10-12-13. Combining Equations (la), (2) and (6) under isentropic constraints! yields P' 7 i + Ap\WA)+i P 7s (7) Thus, according to Equations (6) and (7), the exponent equals B/A + 1. For ideal gases B/A + 1 converges to y. For liquids this quantity varies from a value of 6 for water to a value of 14 for fluorocarbons. The total pressure P comprises internal pressure, Pby which molecules of a medium are bound together, plus the external pressure PH, to which the system is exposed. The term internal pressure includes the physical forces that arise due to pure van der Waals' interactions, the attractive forces that arise due to weak intermolecular bonds like hydrogen bonding, as well as the pressure due to the thermal motion of the individual molcules. The term P, can also be viewed as a negative pressure that a lattice (or a quasi-lattice) of a medium can withstand before it disintegrates to form a new state in which the components' molecules are infinitely apart from one another. Assuming that internal pressure is independent of small changes in external pressure, A?n, one can express P and P' as P = P, + PH (8) and P' = P, + PH + A?H (9) Substituting Equations (8) and (9) in (7) and using the fact that (Ap/p)s is equal to pAPu, where is the adiabatic compressibility of the medium P,+P, AP (10) Adiabatic compressibility p and the ratio of virial coefficients B/A define the properties of the medium. In principle, these parameters can be determined directly by measuring the change in density with pressure under isentropic conditions. However, such measurements can be complex and tedious. Alternatively, these parameters can be measured by using ultrasound, and this is discussed in the next section. Non-linear propagation of acoustic waves in fluids p is related to propagation speed c and density p (see Reference 1) P=l/pc2 (11) Sound speed varies with pressure because of the non-linear nature of sound propagation. The pressure coefficient of sound speed is related to the ratio 156 Ultrasonics 1995 Vol 33 No 2 Determination of molecular properties of liquids: C.M. Expanding the denominator by the Taylor series to quadratic terms and expressing as 1/pc2 Equation (10) reduces to P.+P, H (B/A + 1) (13) Equation (13) establishes a simple relationship between the forces that are operational at the molecular scale with easily measurable acoustic properties of the medium. It is difficult to verify the above equation because P, is not a directly measurable quantity even for simple media. Some of the indirect tests are as follows. Under the limiting condition of ideal gas, P, and converge to zero and the specific heat ratio, y, respectively, and Equation (13) converges to a well-known equation that defines sound speed in ideal gases, i.e. c = (yRT/M) 1/2 (14) where R is the gas constant and M and T are the molecular weight and the temperature of the gas, respectively. In condensed media, the internal pressures are of the order of hundreds or thousands of atmospheres. Therefore, under normal atmospheric conditions Py in Equation (13) can be dropped in comparison with Pi.e. P. = pc- (B/A + 1) (15) which is identical to the equation obtained by Emery et al. by using Tail's equation of state. By using the measured values of B/A (References 11,15,18) and c (References 3,11,16-18) the internal pressures for water, fluoro- carbons, organic liquids and liquid metals were determined (Table 7). On the basis of these results it can be concluded that the internal pressure is the weakest in hydrocarbons. It is about 3-4 times smaller than the internal pressure in pure water. That is, alcohol molecules are about 3-4 times more weakly held together than those of pure water. In liquid metals, on the other hand, the molecules are about an order of magnitude more strongly held together in comparison with water. With this information it is feasible to obtain the work required to transform a liquid to a gaseous state. The thermodynamic state under variable temperature and volume and constant entropy is defined by the work function A. The work done against internal pressure in transforming a liquid to a gaseous state is P.dV (16) where the symbols g and 1 represent gaseous and liquid states of the medium, respectively. Integrating Equation (16) yields A/4. Me2 V, n (B/A + 1) Vc (17) Ultrasonics 1995 Vol 33 No 2 157 Determination of molecular properties of liquids: C.M. Sehgal or AA, Me2 (B/A + 1) -In ergs g (18) where AA(; is the cohesive energy of the material, M is the molecular weight, and Vg and V, are the molar volumes of material in the gaseous and the condensed states, respectively. Equation (18) was used to calculate AAc from the experimentally measured B/A and sound speed in water. These results are shown in Figure 1. In order to check the validity of Equation (18) the AAc values determined from acoustic data were compared with the van der Waals' lattice energy, which was obtained by summing electrical interactions between the molecules, namely, dipole-dipole, dipole-induced dipole and dis- persion. According to Day and Seibin19 the contributions of three interactions are 36.36, 1.92 and 9.0 kJ mole -1 for water. The sum total is 47.28 k J mole"1, which is in excellent agreement with the values of 48.51 kJmole"1 to 44.70 kJ mole -1 obtained from acoustic measurements in the temperature range 273-373 K (Figure /). Similarly determined values of AAc for various aliphatic alcohols and aromatic compounds are also shown in Figure 1. In each case a decrease in AAc with temperature, similar to that of water, is observed. The accuracy of the magnitude of AAc of various organic liquids is more difficult to demonstrate because (a) there is a considerable variation in the acoustic data reported in the literature for such liquids; and (b) to our knowledge the lattice or quasi-lattice energy for such liquids has not been estimated on the basis of electrical interactions. Under such circumstances an approximate test is used to check the validity of Equation (18). If one argues that the internal heat of vaporization, Ly, is a measure of work done against internal pressure in vaporizing a liquid occupying a volume Pj, the AAc should be comparable to LV to a first approximation (neglecting the energy changes required to change the temperature of the medium, or for its expansion). Table 1 lists the values of the latent heat of vaporization and the AA(; estimated from acoustic data by Equation (18). In all the cases, cohesive energy is of the same order of magnitude as the latent heat of vaporization. In view of the large >. 0) 0 0 260 280 300 320 340 360 380 Temperature (K) Figure 1 Cohesive energy as a function of temperature for various pure liquids. The energy values were determined by using the measured sound velocity and the acoustic non-linearity parameter. (a) Water, (b) methanoi, (c) ethanol, (d) propanol, (e) butanol, (f) benzene, (g) chlorobenzene, (h) benzylalcohol, (i) nitrobenzene variations in B/A and c values reported in the literature, and the fact that, according to the theory, AA(; should only be approximately equal to Ly, the agreement between two measurements is exceptionally good. In the literature, a distinction is often made between internal pressure P, and cohesive pressure P^ (see Reference 20). The former is the derivative of energy, a differential quantity whereas the latter is the energy divided by the volume of the medium, i.e. p = -* r -AA or V, pc- (B/A + 1) -In v! . V, (19) (20) It must be noted that the so-called quantity Py in reality represents energy density and has the same units as pressure. This parameter forms the basis of the Hilderbrand solubility parameter20, 0. According to Equation (22) this parameter is defined as <5=P 1/2 PC- ln| V I 1/2 [(B/A +1) \V, (21) The Hildebrand parameter for various liquids was- determined by using acoustic measurements and Equation (21). The computed values are compared in Table I with the values obtained by other methods20. An excellent agreement between the two sets of values is observed, thereby demonstrating the use of non-linear ultrasonics to derive the physical properties of a medium. Estimation of effective van der Waals' constants of liquids Although van der Waals' equation is strictly suitable for gaseous media, it has been used as an approximation for liquids (Reference 16, Chapter 14). Since internal forces in liquids are much greater than the external pressures, van der Waals' equation22 can be represented as P, % RT/(V, - b) and it is also known that22 (22) P, = a/T2 (23) Vf in the above equations represents the molar volume of liquids and a and b are van der Waals' constants. Equations (22) and (23) can be rearranged with the help of Equation (15) to yield a = pc (B/A + 1) b= V, RT 'pc2 (B/A + 1) (24) (25) Thus the constants a and b can be directly determined from acoustic measurements of B/A and c by using the last two equations. These values are summarized in Table 2 for various liquids. In all the cases van der Waals' constants for the condensed state are smaller than their values in the gaseous state. This difference is not surprising and simply confirms the general belief that the inferences drawn on the basis of van der Waals' equation from a very extended gaseous state are not applicable in 158 Ultrasonics 1995 Vol 33 No 2 Determination of molecular properties of liquids: C.M. Sehgal Table 2 Effective van der Waals' parameters for liquids determined from acoustic measurements Liquid S* Gasea s R atios Medium a (Patm.mol"2) b (Imol-1) (Patm.mol 2) (Reference 14) b(Imol-1) a(gas)/a(liq) A(gas)/A(liq) Water 1.1171 0.01135 5.464 0.03049 4.89 2.69 Methanol 1.793 0.01851 9.523 0.06702 5.31 3.62 1.521 0.01458 6.26 4.6 Ethanol 3.659 0.03598 12.02 0.08407 3.29 2.33 3.271 0.03332 3.67 2.52 Propanol 5.692 0.05117 14.92 0.1019 2.62 1.96 5.865 0.05186 2.54 1.93 Acetone 5.817 0.05118 13.91 0.0994 2.39 1.94 Mercury 6.966 0.01404 8.093 0.01696 1.16 1.2 an unmodified form to condensed phases. Assumptions made in van der Waals' equation that lead to the constancy of a and b, (for example the internal pressure varies inversely as the square of the volume; the magnitudes of a and b are unchanged with temperature; and b is independent of internal pressures) are grossly violated when applied to condensed media. Nevertheless, this example illustrates that, by using acoustic measure- ments, effective van der Waals' parameters can be determined. It is often customary to calculate internal pressure in liquids by using van der Waals' constants of gases. This may be misleading because the present results suggest the constants a and b of liquids are several times smaller than the value b in the gaseous state. The constant b is related to the distance of closest approach of molecules, d, by the equation22 b = (2/3)NoI3 (26) where Ny is Avogadro's constant. Eliminating b from Equations (25) and (26) yields d as a function of acoustic parameters Vr RT ^ ,(B/A + 1) I 1/3 (27) By using Equation (27) and the acoustic measurements the value o for liquid water was calculated to be 2.425 A at 20 . The experiments using the X-ray diffraction technique show the distance between the nearest neighbour in liquid water ranges from 2.84 A at 40 to 2.94 A at 200 (see Reference 23). These values are in close agreement with the values obtained from acoustic data. The origin of the small difference in the two values is difficult to trace. It may be partly related to temperature differences and may also result due to the difference in the meaning between 'distance of closest-approach' and 'distance between the nearest neighbour.' The latter obtained from X-ray measurements provides information on the distance between oxygen atoms of the nearest neighbours. The former, on the other hand, is related to a cube root of the free volume not occupied by the molecules. It is not only dependent upon how close the neighbouring molecules are but also on how emcienctly they are packed together. For example, there may be a situation where two systems of identical molecules have equal distance between the nearest neighbour but because, in one case, the packing is more efficient (more molecules are enclosed within the unit volume), less free space is available and, hence, has a smaller distance of closest approach. Therefore, although the distance of closest approach and the distance between the nearest- neighbour are correlated, they are not exactly the same quantities, and may explain the difference between measured and predicted results. Further evidence in support of the measured value of d is obtained by using it to determine the molcular dynamic constants, and comparing their magnitude with the known values. Molecular dynamics: estimation of self-diffusion coefficient and transitional correlation times According to the Stokes-Einstein equation, diffusion D of a spherical particle of radius r in a medium of viscosity r] is D = kT kT /(trans) 67i^r (28) where, /(trans) is the translational frictional coefficient and k is the Boltzmann coefficient. Similarly, correlation time for the rotation of spherical particles is given by the equation /(rot) 47ir]r3 6kT ~ 3kT (29) where /(rot) is the rotational friction coefficient. The last two equations apply to macroscopic systems and assume the surrounding medium to be continuous. It is difficult to justify the fluids to be continuous at a small scale of molecular dimensions. Girer and Wirtz24 calculated the friction exerted upon spherical molecules of radius r by successive spherical shells of thickness 2r^ of the solvent molecules of radius r^ and concluded the frictional coefficients for translation and rotation to be given by /(trans)= 6ni]r\ 1.5-L + r (1 + rJr}_ and /(rot) = 871^ 6rL + L' (1+^L)3), (30) (31) respectively. For pure liquids r = r^, using the effective van der Waals' radius (Equation (27)) of liquids for r, the parameters D and T can be represented by the Ultrasonics 1995 Vol 33 No 2 159 Determination of molecular properties of liquids: C.M. CM I0LU o: o w J T- f^- ("i ^ asi 0 - CT'- OCN r-* LO T- W | lg CO <T- <COr-- 0) CM LO Oi r^ ^-E. 0 0 T- <- <- '-<t (N 0 '~1 CO'" Q ^ CN I 0 <5 0= 0 5 5 esi 0l ^ ʣ ^ sl .'"' \ l.'"^.E [^ 00 T (0 00 00 0 CM CM 't 10 OT-oi <t o co r- p g*- CM CO CO 't (0 . ^' 10' IN eu u ^~- co) '" iu S 0: o " ^si-" '"1 ^ -Ecc r^CDLO tO^t^-CMOOt-LOLO '3;oirooo ^T-oioot^ooi-^tD j.^ o^- (Mcoro^-mcDi^oo ^: - '!Z 0) '- tDCTOO 0)(D ^1' tD 01 ui l l lOOLO 00^- 10 CO T- LnOLOO ( ^ T- ro q [t; ^E i-'CM CMCO 10 r- ^ QJ ^ 5 C tU 0 0 ro(D ^ - Q. ^ -^ 1 1-^OOOTOlOt-CMCO^tLOCDr^ S-i^ equations 'IN^kT D= D T' ^WA +1) PC- 11/3 (32) and 49Nn T pc2 -ABI A + 1) (33) respectively. By using the acoustic non-linearity parameter and the sound speed values in Equations (32) and (33), the diffusion coefficient and rotational correlation times of various liquids were determined at different temperatures. These estimates are listed in Tables 3 and 4: diffusion coefficients determined by the Carr-Purcell nuclear magnetic resonance technique compare closely with the values obtained by the acoustic method. Figure 2 shows the log plots of diffusion coefficients as a function of reciprocal temperature for selected liquids. The solid lines in the plot represent the least squares fit to the data. In all the cases studied, excellent linear fits to the data were observed with r2 in the range of 0.931-0.999. From the slopes of the lines (Arrhenius plots) values of activation energy of diffusion, E(diffusion), were determined and found to be closely correlated to the literature values (Table 3). The rotational correlation times determined by ultrasonic measurements are of the order of picoseconds (Table 4), which is well within the expected range. Also, as one may expect, the correlation times decrease with temperature (Table 4). Conclusions The results of this study illustrate that non-linear ultrasonics can be used to determine the static and 0.0028 0.0030 0.0032 0.0034 1/T(K) 0.0036 0.0038 Figure 2 Arrhenius plots to determine the activation energy for diffusion from acoustic measurements, (a) Water from acoustic measurements; (b) water from N MR measurements; (c) methanoi from acoustic measurements; (d) ethanol from acoustic measure- ments; (e) propanol from acoustic measurements; (f) butanol from acoustic measurements; (g) acetone from acoustic measurements; (h) benzene from acoustic measurements; (i) methyl acetate from acoustic measurements 160 Ultrasonics 1995 Vol 33 No 2 Determination of molecular properties of liquids: C.M. Sehgal Table 4 Estimates of rotational correlation times for various liquids from acoustic measurements Water MeOH EtOH PrO H BuOH Acetone Benzene Methyl acetate Temperature Acoustic Acoustic Acoustic Acoustic Acoustic Acoustic Acoustic Acoustic (K) (PS) (PS) (PS) (PS) (PS) (PS) (PS) (PS) 273 3.084 2.49 9.161 30.381 53.45 283 2.124 1.835 6.935 21.786 38.543 293 1.156 1.361 5.154 15.443 27.359 2.238 2.617 303 1.156 0.966 3.854 10.977 20.205 1.862 5.277 2.237 313 0.898 0.681 2.822 8.307 14.77 1.625 4.467 1.862 323 0.441 6.133 10.998 3.709 333 0.576 3.095 343 2.649 353 0.386 363 373 0.271 the dynamic molecular properties of liquids. Several parameters that define the physical state of the liquid media were derived and compared with the values reported in the literature. In almost all cases a good correlation between values obtained from the acoustic measurements and those reported in the literature were observed. Since ultrasonic measurements of sound speed and B/A are relatively easy to perform on liquids this form of energy offers a simple means of determining the molcular properties of liquids under varying conditions of temperature. In the present study, only pure liquids were considered. By using appropriate mixture laws the analysis can be extended to a more complex media of mixtures. In conclusion the non-linear ultrasonic propa- gation offers an independent means of determining the molecular properties of liquids. References 1 Beyer, R.T. and Letcher, S.V. Physical Ultrasonics (Eds Massey, H.S.W. and Bruecner, K.A.) Academic, NY (1979) Chapter 7, 202-230 2 Hartman, B. Potential energy effects on the sound speed in liquids J Acoust Sue Am (1979) 65 1392-1396 3 Sehgal, C.M., Porter, B.B. and Greenleaf, J.F. Ultrasonic nonlinear parameters of alcohol-water mixtures J Acoust Soc Am (1986) 79 566-570 4 Sehgal, C.M. and Greenleaf, J.F. Correlative study of properties of water in biological system using ultrasound and magnetic resonance Magnetic Resonance in Medicine (1986) 3 976-985 5 Dn, F., Law, W.K. and Frizzell, L.A. Nonlinear ultrasonic wave propagation in biological materials IEEE Ultrasonic Symposium New York (1981) pp. 527-532 6 Yoshiezumi, K., Sato, T. and Ichida, N. A physiochemical of nonlinear parameters B/A for media predominantly composed of water J Acoust Soc Am (1987) 82 302-305 7 Emery, J., Gasse, S. and Dugue, C. Coefficient de non linrit acoustque dans les melanges eau-methanol et eau-ethanol J De Physique Colloque C8 (1979) 40 231-234 8 Sarvazyan, A.P. and Chalikian, T.V. Acoustic nonlinearity parameter B/A of aqueous solutions of some amino acids and proteins J Acoust Soc Am (1990) 88 1555-1561 9 Yang, Y. and Dn, F. Acoustic nonlinearity method for estimating the ratio of bound and free water of biological media Ultrasonics (1993) 31 35-38 10 Beyer, R.T. Parameters of nonlinearity in fluids J Acoust Soc Am (1960) 32 719-721 11 Coppens, A.B., Beyer, R.T. and Ballou. J. Parameter of nonlinearity in fluids. III.
